question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user forge the X-Forwarded-For header to spoof their IP address, bypassing any IP-based rate limiting on staking reward claim endpoints, allowing them to submit an unbounded number of reward-claiming transactions and drain unpaid staking rewards from account 0.0.800?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user set the X-Real-IP header to an arbitrary value since getClientIpAddress() trusts it unconditionally, causing the middleware to log a false identity while the attacker repeatedly queries staking reward endpoints to enumerate pending_reward balances across accounts without being rate-limited or blocked?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a crafted X-Forwarded-For header containing a comma-separated list of IPs (e.g., '10.0.0.1, 127.0.0.1') to confuse IP-based access controls on staking reward APIs, since getClientIpAddress() returns the raw header value without parsing the first untrusted hop, enabling the attacker to appear as a trusted internal address and access staking reward state?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user inject newline characters (CRLF) into the X-Forwarded-For or X-Real-IP header to perform log injection, inserting fake log entries that mask their staking reward theft activity and prevent operators from detecting anomalous reward-claiming patterns in the Rosetta API logs?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request with a malformed X-Real-IP header containing log-format-mimicking content (e.g., '1.2.3.4 GET /network/status 200') to inject false successful log entries, hiding repeated staking reward enumeration requests among fabricated benign-looking log lines?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TracingMiddleware() calls inner.ServeHTTP() before logging, meaning the staking reward handler executes fully before any log entry is written, allowing the attacker to time a burst of reward-claiming requests within a single staking period window without triggering real-time alerting?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: tracingResponseWriter.Write()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where tracingResponseWriter.Write() stores the full response body in w.data in memory, and if the response contains staking reward account details (pending_reward, stake_period_start), a memory disclosure or shared-state bug could expose another user's unpaid staking reward data to the attacker?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user flood the Rosetta API with high-volume requests containing crafted X-Forwarded-For headers, causing log storage exhaustion that rotates or drops logs covering legitimate staking reward claim transactions, enabling the attacker to steal rewards while audit trails are destroyed?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user set both X-Real-IP and X-Forwarded-For headers simultaneously, and since getClientIpAddress() prefers X-Real-IP unconditionally, exploit this priority order to bypass a downstream IP allowlist that only checks X-Forwarded-For, gaining access to staking reward management endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the timing measurement (time.Since(start)) logged by TracingMiddleware() to perform a timing side-channel attack, inferring when the staking period boundary occurs by observing response latency differences in staking reward queries, then timing a reward-claiming transaction to maximize unpaid reward capture?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests to internal paths (livenessPath, metricsPath, readinessPath) that are only logged at debug level, effectively making those requests invisible in production info-level logs, and use the responses to gather staking system health state to time a staking reward theft attack when the system is in a degraded state?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request URI containing staking-related query parameters with embedded special characters that, when logged via fmt.Sprintf in TracingMiddleware(), cause log parsing tools to misinterpret the log entry, hiding the attacker's enumeration of staking reward accounts?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user omit all IP headers (X-Real-IP, X-Forwarded-For) so that getClientIpAddress() falls back to net.SplitHostPort(r.RemoteAddr), and if RemoteAddr is a shared NAT or proxy IP, the attacker's staking reward theft requests are attributed to a shared IP, making individual attribution impossible and preventing blocking?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: tracingResponseWriter.WriteHeader()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request that causes the staking reward handler to call WriteHeader() with a 2xx code but then return an error body, and since tracingResponseWriter.WriteHeader() only stores the code without validating the body, the middleware logs a successful transaction while the actual reward state is inconsistent, masking a double-claim attempt?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a large number of requests to staking reward endpoints with rotating spoofed X-Forwarded-For values, causing TracingMiddleware() to log each request under a different IP, preventing rate-limit aggregation and allowing the attacker to enumerate all accounts with non-zero pending_reward values without triggering per-IP thresholds?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an X-Forwarded-For value of '127.0.0.1' or '::1' to make getClientIpAddress() return a loopback address, causing the middleware to log the request as originating from localhost, bypassing any log-based anomaly detection that filters out internal traffic, while the attacker queries staking reward balances externally?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that internalPaths are logged at debug level (log.Debug) while all other paths use log.Info, by sending staking reward queries to paths that are misclassified as internal, causing those requests to be silently dropped from production logs and making the attacker's reward enumeration invisible?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: tracingResponseWriter.Write()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger concurrent requests to staking reward endpoints such that multiple tracingResponseWriter instances share or race on the w.data field, causing a data race that returns one user's staking reward response body to a different user, leaking pending_reward amounts and enabling targeted theft?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user set X-Forwarded-For to a private RFC1918 address (e.g., '192.168.1.1') to impersonate an internal service, causing getClientIpAddress() to return a trusted-looking IP that bypasses IP-based access controls on staking reward administrative endpoints, allowing the attacker to manipulate pending reward state?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with an extremely long X-Forwarded-For header value to cause log line truncation or buffer overflow in the logging backend, corrupting log entries around staking reward transactions and preventing forensic reconstruction of reward theft activity?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential log-after-serve design of TracingMiddleware() (inner.ServeHTTP runs before logging) to perform a TOCTOU attack where the staking reward state changes between when the handler executes and when the log entry is written, causing the log to reflect a different reward amount than what was actually claimed?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an X-Real-IP header with an IPv6 address that includes a zone ID (e.g., 'fe80::1%eth0') which net.SplitHostPort does not handle correctly, causing getClientIpAddress() to return a malformed string that breaks IP-based rate limiting and allows unlimited staking reward queries?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request path that matches a staking reward endpoint but includes URL-encoded characters that, when logged via request.URL.RequestURI(), appear as a different path in log analysis tools, hiding the true nature of the staking reward enumeration from security monitoring?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests through a chain of proxies with a crafted X-Forwarded-For header like 'victim-ip, attacker-ip' so that getClientIpAddress() returns the first value (victim-ip), attributing the attacker's staking reward theft requests to the victim's IP address and causing the victim to be blocked or blamed?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TracingMiddleware() logs the full request URI including query parameters, and if staking reward endpoints accept account IDs or reward amounts as query parameters, the attacker can enumerate valid account IDs by analyzing log-reflected error responses without needing direct database access?
